elements.sent-2411-001: The widespread adoption of deep learning across various industries has introduced substantial challenges, particularly in terms of model explainability and security.
elements.sent-2411-002: The inherent complexity of deep learning models, while contributing to their effectiveness, also renders them susceptible to adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-003: Among these, backdoor attacks are especially concerning, as they involve surreptitiously embedding specific triggers within training data, causing the model to exhibit aberrant behavior when presented with input containing the triggers.
elements.sent-2411-004: Such attacks often exploit vulnerabilities in outsourced processes, compromising model integrity without affecting performance on clean (trigger-free) input data.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we present a comprehensive review of existing mitigation strategies designed to counter backdoor attacks in image recognition.
elements.sent-2411-006: We provide an in-depth analysis of the theoretical foundations, practical efficacy, and limitations of these approaches.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, we conduct an extensive benchmarking of sixteen state-of-the-art approaches against eight distinct backdoor attacks, utilizing three datasets, four model architectures, and three poisoning ratios.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results, derived from 122,236 individual experiments, indicate that while many approaches provide some level of protection, their performance can vary considerably.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, when compared to two seminal approaches, most newer approaches do not demonstrate substantial improvements in overall performance or consistency across diverse settings.
elements.sent-2411-010: Drawing from these findings, we propose potential directions for developing more effective and generalizable defensive mechanisms in the future.
elements.sent-2411-011: In recent years, deep learning has seen remarkable advancements, driving its widespread adoption across diverse industries and academic fields.
elements.sent-2411-012: This rapid integration is evident in sectors such as healthcare, education, automotive, and logistics, where deep learning is increasingly utilized to foster innovation~\cite{pouyanfar2018survey .}
elements.sent-2411-013: A key factor in the success of deep learning is its ability to extract complex patterns from data.
elements.sent-2411-014: While this capability offers significant advantages, it also introduces substantial challenges related to explainability.
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite their strong predictive performance, deep learning models often lack transparency and interpretability, making it difficult to provide clear reasoning for specific predictions.
elements.sent-2411-016: The black-box nature of deep learning models has been shown to expose them to considerable security vulnerabilities~\cite{wang2019security .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, classification models, such as those used in image recognition, have been demonstrated to be vulnerable to manipulation, with multiple instances of adversarial attacks successfully compromising their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, the seminal work of~\cite{szegedy2013intriguing highlights the vulnerability of image classification models to adversarial examples, showing that imperceptible perturbations applied to input images can induce significant misclassifications.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The iconic ``Panda-Gibbon'' image, created using the method proposed in~\cite{szegedy2013intriguing for generating adversarial perturbations, is a striking illustration of the fragility inherent in deep learning models, despite their sophistication.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Since then, several other adversarial threats have been identified, affecting a wide range of learning tasks~\cite{wang2019security .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In real-world scenarios, backdoor attacks represent a significant threat, especially in scenarios where classification outputs drive automated decision-making processes~\cite{gu2017badnets .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Backdoor attacks intentionally establish a relationship between a spurious feature within the input space, known as a trigger, and a specific classification outcome.
elements.sent-2411-023: Once this association is established, a compromised model will classify clean images (i.e., unaltered images) and backdoor images (i.e., images containing the trigger) differently.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a result, backdoor attacks undermine the integrity of a model's decision-making by inserting an unwanted behaviour, referred to as a backdoor task, without compromising its ability to correctly perform the original classification task of recognizing clean images~\cite{li2022backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In Figure~\ref{fig:backdoor-example , we present both a clean image and a backdoor version, along with the classification results from a successfully compromised (backdoored) model.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This backdoor attack can be practically executed by simply placing a yellow sticker on a stop sign.
elements.sent-2411-027: In an autonomous driving context, such an attack can have severe safety implications.

elements.sent-2411-028: To successfully execute a backdoor attack on a model, the adversary must compromise the training pipeline of the target model.
elements.sent-2411-029: This makes backdoor attacks particularly dangerous when model training is outsourced to third parties, such as through cloud-based Machine-Learning-as-a-Service platforms~\cite{liu2019abs .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In such cases, an adversary can manipulate the training data or procedure, injecting backdoor tasks without the victim's awareness.
elements.sent-2411-031: The widespread use of pre-trained model weights in the deep learning community exacerbates this threat.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, a recent industry survey~\cite{grosse2023towards has revealed that 48.1\% of participants utilize third-party weights for model training.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Moreover, popular machine learning platforms such as Hugging Face\footnote{https://huggingface.co/ allow users to download thousands of off-the-shelf pre-trained models, further amplifying the potential risk of backdoor attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-034: To address the challenges posed by the hard-to-explain nature of current deep learning approaches, machine learning security has emerged as a critical area of research~\cite{yuan2019adversarial .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Although it does not directly solve the problem of explainability, machine learning security seeks to develop new methods to protect against known threats, ensuring that deep learning can be safely deployed.
elements.sent-2411-036: Within this field, an emerging body of work focuses on developing defensive strategies specifically designed to counter backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-037: These strategies aim to mitigate risks by removing the backdoor task from a model while preserving its ability to classify clean inputs.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, despite significant advancements in this field, the consistency and reliability of current proposals across diverse settings remain uncertain due to practical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-039: Many proposed approaches are evaluated using a limited scope of attacks, datasets, model architectures, and data availability conditions.
elements.sent-2411-040: This raises questions about their generalizability and effectiveness in diverse real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this work, we critically analyse existing research on backdoor mitigation, distinguishing it from other defensive approaches such as identifying poisoned training examples or synthesizing backdoored inputs.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our analysis is centered on works designed for image classification, although backdoor attacks are also relevant in other applications such as natural language processing~\cite{sheng2022survey and other computer vision tasks such as object detection~\cite{chan2022baddet and semantic segmentation~\cite{li2021hidden .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We conduct a comprehensive survey and critical analysis of existing mitigation approaches.
elements.sent-2411-044: Unlike other surveys on this topic, we offer a detailed summary of the prevalent approaches used to address backdoor attacks, along with their main assumptions and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-045: Furthermore, we experimentally evaluate the majority of the discussed works against a diverse range of backdoor attacks, covering eight distinct types.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our evaluations, comprising 122,236 individual experiments, spanning three different datasets, four model architectures, and three distinct data availability settings.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our benchmarking results reveal several key findings that offer valuable insights to inform future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-048: Existing Surveys and Benchmarks: There exist several surveys on backdoor attack mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-049: We provide a comparative summary of these works in Table~\ref{tab:survey-compare .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Most of these surveys take a broader approach than ours, which often results in a less detailed analysis of the methods currently employed within the image classification domain.
elements.sent-2411-051: For instance, \cite{sheng2022survey , \cite{cheng2023backdoor , and \cite{zhao2024survey concentrate on language tasks, while~\cite{le2024comprehensive and \cite{yan2023backdoor focus on face and voice recognition tasks, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, \cite{wan2024data specifically surveys mitigation strategies devised for federated learning.}

elements.sent-2411-061: Fundamental to any training process is the use of a loss function.
elements.sent-2411-062: Within image classification, cross-entropy loss, denoted as $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{CE }$, is most commonly used.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Using the predicted softmax probability vector $a$ for a given input $x$ and its corresponding one-hot true label vector $y$, $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{CE }$ associated with this data instance is expressed as \begin{equation}\label{ce-loss:single} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CE}}(x, y, \theta) = -y^\intercal \log(a).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In addition, the cross-entropy loss for a dataset $(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}$ is calculated as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CE}}(\mathcal{D}, \theta) =- \frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}|} \sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}} y^\intercal \log(a). \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: A neural network is a parameterized function $f(x,\theta)$ that maps an input $x$ to an output $y$, where $\theta$ represents the network's parameters.
elements.sent-2411-066: In an $m$-class classification task, the output consists of softmax probabilities for each class.
elements.sent-2411-067: Given a training dataset $\mathcal{D _{t}$, $\theta$ is optimized to minimize an aggregate loss function, typically $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{CE }$ for image classification, as \begin{equation}\label{general-training-objective} \min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CE}}(\mathcal{D}_{t}, \theta).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Stochastic gradient descent or its variants is commonly used for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-069: Neural network architectures vary widely, with convolutional neural networks (CNNs) being prevalent for image classification.
elements.sent-2411-070: CNNs can often be decomposed into a feature extractor and a linear classifier.
elements.sent-2411-071: Letting $\varphi$ and $\omega$ represent the parameters of these components, respectively, we have $\omega \cup \varphi \subseteq \theta$ and $\omega \cap \varphi = \emptyset$.

elements.sent-2411-073: A critical consideration in this process is the poisoning ratio, which represents the proportion of backdoor to clean data (i.e., $\frac{\|\mathcal{D _{b}\| {\|\mathcal{D _{c}\|}$), balancing the trade-off between the attack's stealth and effectiveness.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The clean dataset $\mathcal{D _{c}$ consists of the original unaltered inputs $x$ and their associated labels $y$.}

elements.sent-2411-086: Trigger characteristics pertain to the type of trigger $\rho$ used by the adversary, encompassing coverage, consistency and modification mode, as defined in~\cite{wu2022backdoorbench .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Below, we briefly discuss these characteristics with reference to the works listed in Table~\ref{tab:attack-details .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Trigger coverage refers to the extent of modification when $\rho$ is integrated into the $x$ by $B$~\cite{wu2022backdoorbench .}
elements.sent-2411-089: In Figure~\ref{fig:backdoor-example2 , we provide examples of backdoor images generated by the considered attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Coverage is typically classified as global or local.
elements.sent-2411-091: Global coverage implies that $\rho$ affects a significant portion of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-092: For instance, the Blended attack~\cite{chen2017targeted alters $x$ by incorporating a trigger image (e.g., a picture of Hello Kitty) with a blending ratio that controls its transparency.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In contrast, local triggers modify only a small portion of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Among the attacks considered, BadNets~\cite{gu2017badnets and IAB~\cite{nguyen2020input utilize local triggers.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In the case of BadNets, a small $n \times n$ pixel pattern (e.g., a $3 \times 3$ white square) is inserted into images at a fixed position (e.g., at the bottom left-hand corner).
elements.sent-2411-096: Trigger consistency refers to whether the same $\rho$ is used across $\mathcal{D _{b}$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: When $\rho$ is fixed, the attack is deemed static.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, the BadNets attack inserts the same $n \times n$ pixel pattern into the same position in each image.
elements.sent-2411-099: However, the success of works such as \cite{wang2019neural in identifying static triggers used by BadNets has led to the preference for dynamic triggers in recent works.}
elements.sent-2411-100: More specifically, dynamic methods make $\rho$ dependent on $x$.
elements.sent-2411-101: For example, IAB generates a unique $\rho$ for each $(x, y) \in \mathcal{D _{c}$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Unlike other dynamic methods such as SSBA, BPP, and LIRA, which synthesize barely perceptible patterns, IAB generates patterns similar to those used in BadNets.
elements.sent-2411-103: Moreover, the triggers generated by IAB for different images are designed to be non-reusable, meaning the trigger for $x$ does not work for $x'$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Trigger modification mode refers to how $\rho$ is applied to $x$.
elements.sent-2411-105: The two most common modes are additive and replacement.
elements.sent-2411-106: Attacks using the replacement mode substitute parts of $x$ with $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-107: For example, BadNets replaces the pixels in the $n \times n$ region of $x$ that overlaps with $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-108: The exception to this categorization is the WaNet attack, where the elastic image warping method utilized in this attack cannot be classified as either additive or replacement.
elements.sent-2411-109: The landscape of defense against backdoor attacks encompasses several sub-problems, addressed by different works.
elements.sent-2411-110: For instance, some works propose methods that identify backdoor samples within the poisoned dataset $\widetilde{\mathcal{D _{t}$ \cite{gao2019strip .}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Another common task is backdoor synthesis, which involves generating the backdoor dataset $\mathcal{D _{b}$ from the clean dataset $\mathcal{D _{c}$ given the model parameters $\theta$~\cite{liu2019abs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Such works attempt to model the trigger distribution used by the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-113: In this work, we concentrate on backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-114: Distinct from other defensive strategies, backdoor mitigation seeks to remove backdoor behavior from the model while preserving its original classification capability~\cite{liu2018finepruning .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Although several works address backdoor mitigation, inconsistencies often arise concerning the threat model considered by each work.
elements.sent-2411-116: To ensure a fair comparison, we focus on proposals that adopt a set of assumptions consistent with the strongest adversarial scenario, specifically, the Outsourced Training threat model outlined in section~\ref{prelim:backdoor-attacks .}

elements.sent-2411-122: When backdoor attacks were first introduced for image classification, \cite{gu2017badnets hypothesized that backdoored models extract two distinct feature sets.}
elements.sent-2411-123: They posited that certain model components, such as convolutional and dense layers, become associated either with the main task or the backdoor task.
elements.sent-2411-124: Consequently, when the model is presented with $\hat{x}$, the components linked to the backdoor task contribute to its classification as $\hat{y}$ rather than $y$.
elements.sent-2411-125: In Figure~\ref{fig:model-segmentation , we visually represent how a model can be decomposed according to this hypothesis.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Historically model pruning has been employed to identify and remove redundant model components, thereby improving inference efficiency~\cite{he2023structured .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Building upon the hypothesis that model components can be segmented as described, several studies have explored the application of model pruning for backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-128: Pruning-based approaches aim to identify and eliminate model components associated with backdoor behavior.
elements.sent-2411-129: To achieve this, various strategies have been adopted, including metric-based, masking-based, and additive techniques.
elements.sent-2411-130: In this section, we provide a comprehensive review of these subcategories, along with a comparative analysis of the approaches within them.

elements.sent-2411-131: Works adopting a metric-based approach aim to directly quantify the contribution of each model component to the backdoor task.
elements.sent-2411-132: By applying a defined metric, these works distinguish between clean and backdoor components based on their respective metric values.
elements.sent-2411-133: The work of \cite{liu2018finepruning is considered a pioneering effort in backdoor mitigation.}
elements.sent-2411-134: In their initial investigation, \cite{liu2018finepruning compare the channel-wise average activation produced by $\mathcal{D _{c}$ and $\mathcal{D _{b}$ in the final convolutional layer.}}}

elements.sent-2411-236: Model pruning is an important strategy in mitigating backdoor attacks, building on the hypothesis that neural networks can be decomposed into distinct components responsible for either clean or backdoor tasks.
elements.sent-2411-237: By selectively pruning the components related to backdoor behaviors, researchers aim to restore model integrity while preserving performance on the original task.
elements.sent-2411-238: Metric-based pruning approaches quantify each model component's contribution to backdoor behavior through various metrics.
elements.sent-2411-239: Approaches like FP remove filters based on their activation patterns, while BNP utilizes distribution statistics to identify backdoor components.
elements.sent-2411-240: CLP's Lipschitz-based approach uses approximations of the channel Lipschitz constants to guide pruning decisions.
elements.sent-2411-241: Masking-based pruning techniques optimize a parameter mask to remove backdoor functionality.
elements.sent-2411-242: Approaches such as ANP, AWM, and RNP employ optimization frameworks to iteratively refine the mask, targeting backdoor components while retaining model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-243: Lastly, additive pruning approaches introduce new components into the network, functioning as filters that mitigate backdoor influences without directly removing existing filters.
elements.sent-2411-244: These approaches exemplify innovative ways of addressing backdoor mitigation while maintaining the overall structure of the model.
elements.sent-2411-245: Each pruning approach offers distinct advantages and limitations that require robust evaluation across a broad range of settings.
elements.sent-2411-246: An alternative approach to model pruning for backdoor mitigation is fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-247: Instead of removing a subset of $\theta$, fine-tuning methods adjust the values of $\theta$ to eliminate the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-248: A key aspect of these methods is the objective function, which typically incorporates one or more carefully designed regularisation terms.
elements.sent-2411-249: These terms are usually selected based on specific insights gained from preliminary investigations.
elements.sent-2411-250: In this section, we review the most prominent fine-tuning approaches, categorized into distinct subgroups based on their unique methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-251: The first subcategory of fine-tuning methods follows a more conventional approach.
elements.sent-2411-252: Here, conventional refers to the proposed optimization problem being closely aligned with~\eqref{general-training-objective .}
elements.sent-2411-253: In their preliminary investigation, \cite{min2024fst evaluate the effectiveness of minimisation~\eqref{general-training-objective given $\mathcal{D _{m}$ for mitigating backdoor tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Their analysis decomposes $\theta$ into $\varphi$, parameters associated with the feature extractor, and $\omega$, parameters associated with the linear classifier.
elements.sent-2411-255: They tested fine-tuning various combinations of $\varphi$ and $\omega$, concluding that the minimisation~\eqref{general-training-objective for any combination of these parameters was largely ineffective in removing backdoors (see \cite[Table 1]{min2024fst ).}}

elements.sent-2411-257: To enhance this process, they introduce a regularisation term that encourages divergence between the original and new values of $\omega$, referred to as $\hat{\omega}$ and $\omega$, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-276: Inspired by its success in other learning settings, two proposals explore how knowledge distillation (KD) can be leveraged for backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-277: Traditionally used to transfer knowledge from larger to smaller models, KD has been effectively applied in tasks such as image classification \cite{gou2021knowledge .}
elements.sent-2411-278: In the context of backdoor mitigation, the distillation process is reframed as a knowledge filtering task.
elements.sent-2411-279: Instead of transferring all knowledge from the original model, the goal of knowledge filtering is to distill only the information relevant to the clean task, thereby eliminating the backdoor-related information.
elements.sent-2411-280: A common approach to KD involves a teacher-student architecture.
elements.sent-2411-281: In typical applications, the teacher model is a larger, more capable model whose knowledge is transferred to a smaller student model.
elements.sent-2411-282: However, in the case of backdoor mitigation, access to a non-backdoored teacher model is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-283: Subsequently, approaches that employ this method must overcome this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-284: To implement KD, \cite{li2021nad introduce a new attention-based method.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Rather than relying on feature maps (i.e., intermediate activation outputs) to perform KD, \cite{li2021nad suggest using attention maps.}
elements.sent-2411-286: These maps compress the channel dimension of the feature maps, utilizing an attention operator $\mathcal{A}$, which maps from $\mathbb{R ^{c \times h \times w}$ to $\mathbb{R ^{h \times w}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: They propose the following two variants \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{sum}}^{p}(x, \theta, l) = \sum_{i=1}^{c} |f_{l,i}(x,\theta)|^{p}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{mean}}^{p}(x, \theta, l) = \frac{1}{c} \sum_{i=1}^{c} |f_{l,i}(x,\theta)|^{p}\end{equation} where $f_{l,i}$ is the $i^{\text{th }$ channel activation of $x$ at the $l^{\text{th }$ layer and $p > 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: To perform KD within the teacher-student framework, \cite{li2021nad first fine-tune $\theta$ using $\mathcal{D _{m}$ resulting in a teacher model with parameters $\theta_{T}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: The student's parameters, $\theta_{S}$, are the original parameters $\theta$ that have not been fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-290: The KD is then performed by comparing the activation maps between the teacher and student models.
elements.sent-2411-291: To achieve this, \cite{li2021nad use $\mathcal{A _{sum ^{p}$ to define a distillation loss as \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{NAD}}(x, \theta_{T}, \theta_{S}, l) = \left\| \frac{\mathcal{A}(x, \theta_{T}, l)}{\|\mathcal{A}(x, \theta_{T}, l)\|_{2}} - \frac{\mathcal{A}(x, \theta_{S}, l)}{\|\mathcal{A}(x, \theta_{S}, l)\|_{2}}\right\|_{2},\end{equation} and solve the following optimization problem \begin{equation}\min_{\theta_{S}} \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{CE}(\mathcal{D}_{m}, \theta_{S}) + \frac{\lambda}{|\mathcal{D}_{m}|} \sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{D}_{m}} \sum_{l=1}^{L} \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{NAD}(x, \theta_{T}, \theta_{S},l),\end{equation} where $\lambda$ controls the contribution of the distillation loss.}}}

elements.sent-2411-365: Fine-tuning, as an alternative to model pruning for backdoor mitigation, adjusts the parameters of a model rather than removing them.
elements.sent-2411-366: This strategy is typically governed by an objective function incorporating tailored regularization terms.
elements.sent-2411-367: Conventional fine-tuning approaches, such as FST and FT-SAM, attempt to adjust model weights to eliminate backdoors, though they often struggle with escaping local minima and thus fail to fully mitigate the backdoor threat.
elements.sent-2411-368: More advanced approaches based on KD, such as NAD and BCU, reframe fine-tuning as a process of filtering out harmful information.
elements.sent-2411-369: These approaches leverage the distillation of knowledge from a teacher model to a student model to effectively mitigate backdoor attacks while retaining the model's performance on clean data.
elements.sent-2411-370: Additionally, approaches such as MESA and BAERASER employ surrogate data generation to support the fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-371: However, these approaches, rely on the defender having prior knowledge about the specific trigger used by an adversary.
elements.sent-2411-372: To remove this assumption, approaches such as PBE, i-BAU, and SAU modify existing adversarial training techniques.
elements.sent-2411-373: Similar to pruning approaches, each fine-tuning approach presents unique strengths and weaknesses, necessitating thorough evaluation across a diverse range of settings to fully understand their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-374: In this section, we describe the experimental setup of our extensive evaluations covering 16 of the 18 approaches discussed in Sections~\ref{model-pruning and ~\ref{fine-tuning .}}
elements.sent-2411-375: We benchmark each approach across a wide variety of settings as our evaluations span various backdoor attacks, model architectures, datasets, and poisoning ratios, resulting in a total of 288 distinct attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-376: Moreover, unlike \cite{wu2022backdoorbench , we test each considered mitigation approach across three data availability settings, leading to 122,236 individual experiments in total.}
elements.sent-2411-377: For our evaluations, we employ the BackdoorBench toolkit~\cite{wu2022backdoorbench , as it provided most of the required functionality.}
elements.sent-2411-378: However, we have made several key modifications to this toolkit, such as incorporating the implementation of five additional mitigation approaches.

elements.sent-2411-394: We consider three datasets: CIFAR-10, German Traffic Sign Recognition Benchmark (GTSRB), and Tiny-ImageNet, containing 10, 43, and 200 classes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-395: To evaluate the effect of data availability on the performance of each approach, we assess then under three data settings, based on the sample per class (SPC) value.
elements.sent-2411-396: Specifically, we evaluate each approach considering SPC values of 2, 10, and 100.
elements.sent-2411-397: For each data setting, we conduct 10 iterations, with each iteration utilizing a different random data partition.
elements.sent-2411-398: Moreover, we employ four model architectures: PreAct-ResNet18 (ResNet), VGG-19 with batch normalisation (VGG), EfficientNet-B3 (EfficientNet), and MobileNetV3-Large (MobileNet), using their default configurations as provided by BackdoorBench.
elements.sent-2411-399: To evaluate the effectiveness of backdoor mitigation, we use three key performance measures, commonly used in the literature: clean accuracy (ACC), attack success rate (ASR), and recovery accuracy (RA).
elements.sent-2411-400: These metrics, though sometimes referred to by different names in various works, serve the same purposes:.
elements.sent-2411-401: ACC represents the accuracy of the original classification task.
elements.sent-2411-402: It is measured as the accuracy of the model on the testing data without the backdoor trigger applied (i.e., the clean data).
elements.sent-2411-403: ASR measures the effectiveness of the backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-404: It is calculated as the accuracy on testing data with the backdoor trigger applied (i.e., the backdoor data) and corresponding labels changed to the target label.
elements.sent-2411-405: Note that, testing data that originally belongs to the target class is omitted from this calculation.
elements.sent-2411-406: RA quantifies how effective a mitigation approach is at restoring the model's classification performance after backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-407: It measures the accuracy of testing data with the backdoor trigger applied but using the original (correct) labels.
elements.sent-2411-408: In essence, ASR indicates whether the application of the trigger results in targeted misclassification, while RA shows whether the model can correctly classify backdoor samples after mitigation is applied, restoring them to their original labels.
elements.sent-2411-409: In our evaluations, we use normalised variants of ACC and RA, while ASR remains unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-410: These normalized metrics provide a clearer understanding of the model’s performance before and after the application of backdoor mitigation approaches.
elements.sent-2411-411: To quantify the impact of mitigation on the accuracy of the original classification task, we take into account the accuracy values before and after mitigation, denoted as $\text{ACC _{\text{pre }$ and $\text{ACC _{\text{post }$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-412: Therefore, we calculate the accuracy reduction ratio (ARR) as \begin{equation}\label{arr-equation} \text{ARR} = \frac{\text{ACC}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ACC}_{\text{post}}}{\text{ACC}_{\text{pre}}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-413: Dividing the difference by the pre-mitigation accuracy accounts for variations in $\text{ACC _{\text{pre }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-414: For instance, most Tiny-ImageNet models exhibit lower $\text{ACC _{\text{pre }$ compared to their CIFAR-10 counterparts.}}
elements.sent-2411-415: Ideally, this ratio approaches $0$, indicating minimal reduction in accuracy due to mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-416: To measure the effectiveness of a mitigation strategy in restoring the classification of backdoor samples to their original classes, we calculate the recovery difference ratio (RDR) as \begin{equation}\label{rdr-equation} \text{RDR} = \frac{\text{ACC}_{\text{pre}} - \text{RA}_{\text{post}}}{\text{ACC}_{\text{pre}}}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-518: Most of the evaluated approaches exhibit highly variable performance when tested across a broader set of scenarios compared to those originally considered in the respective papers.
elements.sent-2411-519: Although it is unrealistic to expect any particular approach to have invariant performance, the variance observed in most approaches is considerable.
elements.sent-2411-520: Specifically, ASR and RDR show significant variability in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-521: Among the evaluated variables, data availability, attack type, and dataset have the largest impact on performance.
elements.sent-2411-522: For data availability, fine-tuning approaches are notably affected when SPC is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-523: Particularly, the performance of the original classification task, as quantified by ARR, deteriorates with reduced SPC.
elements.sent-2411-524: This is a significant observation, as a substantial decrease in ARR can render an approach impractical for real-world applications, irrespective of the removal of the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-525: The inherent complexity of the optimization problems proposed by each fine-tuning approach often results in low bias but high variance, which increases the likelihood of overfitting to $\mathcal{D _{m}$ when SPC is reduced.}
elements.sent-2411-526: Although many approaches attempt to alleviate this issue by constraining the weight or input perturbation space or by defining a multi-objective optimization problem where one objective is to preserve the performance on $\mathcal{D _{m}$, these approaches do not effectively constraint $\theta$ to account for overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-527: Although FST constrains the solution space of $\theta$, its minimisation of the inner product encourages differences among parameters.
elements.sent-2411-528: In contrast, while FT-SAM restricts the perturbation applied to $\theta$, the outer minimisation imposes no direct constraint to $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-529: Given that a practical backdoor mitigation procedure typically operates under the assumption of limited access to clean data, it is crucial for future fine-tuning methods to develop effective strategies that constrain $\theta$ to mitigate the risk of overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-530: By achieving this, we posit that the mitigation task will become more tractable and better positioned to address the bias-variance trade-off while maintaining the performance of the original classification task.
elements.sent-2411-531: Regarding attack type, dataset, and model architecture, most approaches show variable performance in removing and restoring the backdoor task, as measured by ASR and RDR.
elements.sent-2411-532: Unlike ARR, both ASR and RDR are inaccessible to the defender after mitigation, making large uncertainty in these measures a significant risk for practical use.
elements.sent-2411-533: The sensitivity of the performance of most approaches to the values of their hyperparameters is a major factor contributing to this variability.
elements.sent-2411-534: While some approaches evaluate this sensitivity in different settings (cf.
elements.sent-2411-535: Table~\ref{tab:additional-analysis ), the majority of evaluations are often limited in extent due to practical constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-536: This underscores that although hyperparameters are often unavoidable, their inclusion requires careful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-537: Unlike traditional deep learning applications, using validation datasets to find optimal hyperparameter values is impractical for two main reasons.
elements.sent-2411-538: First, as mentioned earlier, ASR and RDR are not observable by the defender in practice, making hyperparameter tuning effective only for minimising ARR.
elements.sent-2411-539: Second, the use of a validation dataset further limits the available mitigation data, which can exacerbate the impact of data availability on overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-540: Another factor contributing to performance variance across attack types, datasets, and model architectures is the reliance on limited observational evidence.
elements.sent-2411-541: Specifically, most backdoor mitigation strategies are built upon analyzing backdoored models and characterizing their behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-542: For instance, CLP investigates the correlation between UCLC and TAC, employing UCLC to prune filters with outlier values.
elements.sent-2411-543: However, our benchmarking results indicate that none of these observations can be deemed universal characteristics associated with backdoor attacks given the varied performance of most proposals.
elements.sent-2411-544: Rather, these observations are likely indicative of backdoor attacks within the specific settings tested by each approach.
elements.sent-2411-545: While we recognise the practical implications of examining the range of settings evaluated in this work, our findings underscore the importance of considering variations in attack type, dataset, and model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-546: Future mitigation approaches ought to carefully account for the impact of limited data availability on performance.
elements.sent-2411-547: Specifically, a more deliberate consideration of the bias-variance trade-off is essential when designing optimisation-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-548: In addition, the inclusion of hyperparameters demands careful thought, as their values can significantly affect practical performance.
elements.sent-2411-549: Since hyperparameter optimization is often challenging or impractical in real-world scenarios, sensitivity to hyperparameter values becomes even more critical in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-550: Finally, future investigations into the underlying mechanisms that drive backdoor attacks (assuming such mechanisms exist) need to carefully account for variations in attack type, dataset, and model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-551: Among the surveyed works, we find that only the proposers of SAU evaluate the ability of their mitigation approach to restore the classification of the backdoor task, quantified as RDR in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-552: While ASR measures how effectively the backdoor induces the misclassification of samples containing the trigger to the target class, BackdoorBench highlights that this measure alone does not determine the overall effectiveness of a mitigation approach.
elements.sent-2411-553: Specifically, minimizing ASR without a corresponding increase in RA, the performance measure used to calculate RDR, is not indicative of optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-554: If ASR is minimised but RA remains low, the model is still unable to accurately classify samples containing the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-555: Although this may not align with the adversary's original objective, it still has significant implications for models deployed in real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-556: Across the tested settings, RDR varies significantly.
elements.sent-2411-557: Even though FT-SAM and SAU are state-of-the-art approaches, their RDR performance still exhibit notable variability.
elements.sent-2411-558: While RDR is dependent on ASR and ARR, as $\text{ASR + \text{RA \leq 1$ and $\text{RA \lessapprox \text{ACC}$, the median and variance of RDR often exceed the values expected from the relationship between these performance measures (see Figs.~\ref{fig:result-summary and \ref{fig:result-summary-2 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-559: Since samples containing the trigger are inaccessible to the defender, restoring the classification of the backdoor task presents a major challenge.
elements.sent-2411-560: Despite efforts by the proposers of approaches NPD, AWN, NC, MESA, BAERASER, PBE, i-BAU, and SAU to model the trigger distribution, a substantial improvement in RDR has not been observed.
elements.sent-2411-561: Therefore, moving forward, more focused exploration of alternative methodologies targeting RDR is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-562: Modelling the trigger distribution is a widely adopted technique, used in nearly half of the surveyed works (cf.. Table~\ref{tab:additional-analysis ).}
elements.sent-2411-563: Approaches that utilize this technique rely on the insights derived from the trigger model to mitigate corresponding backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-564: A key feature shared among these approaches is the use of a constrained optimisation to determine $\delta$, though the specific implementation details differ across approaches.
elements.sent-2411-565: This constrained optimisation requires selecting a norm and an upperbound ($\epsilon$) for the norm of $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-566: This inherently involves certain assumptions about $\rho$, the actual trigger employed by the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-567: Among the discussed approaches, NC, MESA, and BAERASER constrain the $\ell_1$ norm of $\delta$, while NPD, AWN, PBE, i-BAU, and SAU utilize the $\ell_2$ norm.
elements.sent-2411-568: In the evaluated attacks, triggers employed by the BadNets and, arguably, IAB are sparse in nature, whereas other attacks apply smoother, less perceptible triggers.
elements.sent-2411-569: Interestingly, our results suggest that using an $\ell_1$ norm constraint in trigger modelling does not significantly enhance mitigation performance against attacks with sparse triggers compared to using an $\ell_2$ norm constraint.
elements.sent-2411-570: Furthermore, we observe that constraining the $\ell_2$ norm does not guarantee successful mitigation against attacks with smoother triggers, as illustrated by the results for the Blended and SSBA attacks in section~\ref{results:attack .}
elements.sent-2411-571: One of the main challenges with using the $\ell_2$ norm is the natural occurrence of adversarial examples within the input space.
elements.sent-2411-572: To tackle this, AWN and i-BAU model $\delta$ as a global input perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-573: However, the results for AWN and i-BAU indicate that this additional constraint leads to suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-574: In contrast, NPD, PBE, and SAU adopt a sample-specific approach to modelling the trigger distribution.
elements.sent-2411-575: NPD assigns a unique $\delta$ for each $x$ based on its second-largest logit, ensuring that the perturbation $\delta$ causes targeted misclassification.
elements.sent-2411-576: However, our findings in section~\ref{results:attack reveal that this selection method yields variable performance.}
elements.sent-2411-577: Conversely, PBE employs an existing untargeted adversarial example generation method, demonstrating that untargeted adversarial examples generated using the PGD attack tend to exhibit biased classification towards the adversary's target class.
elements.sent-2411-578: However, our findings suggest that the error rate of the PGD attack (i.e., the proportion of adversarial examples not classified as the target class) impacts PBE's performance, particularly in scenarios with limited data availability.
elements.sent-2411-579: To account for the presence of adversarial examples in sample-specific trigger modelling with $\ell_2$ norm constraint, SAU filters candidate triggers using the original model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-580: Specifically, SAU identifies sample-specific perturbations $\delta$ that induce consistent misclassification given the original model parameters $\theta$ and the modified ones $\bar{\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-581: Our results show that this strategy more effectively distinguishes between adversarial examples and candidate triggers, as evidenced by robust ASR performance across the tested attacks.
elements.sent-2411-582: However, it is important to note that this approach alone is insufficient for restoring the correct classification of backdoor samples, as discussed earlier (see section~\ref{discussion:ra ).}
elements.sent-2411-583: We critically evaluated various state-of-the-art backdoor attack mitigation strategies within the context of image recognition.
elements.sent-2411-584: Our analysis, spanning a broad spectrum of attacks, datasets, model architectures, data availabilities, and poisoning ratios uncovers several key insights into the effectiveness and limitations of current approaches:.
elements.sent-2411-585: While many approaches demonstrate strong performance in specific settings, most exhibit significant variability, particularly when faced with diverse attack types and constrained data availability.
elements.sent-2411-586: Pruning-based approaches such as BNP, ANP, and CLP offer a degree of robustness but struggle with performance consistency.
elements.sent-2411-587: Fine-tuning approaches, notably FT-SAM and SAU, show promise by outperforming their respective baselines, though they come with trade-offs, especially in terms of accuracy reduction and recovery performance.
elements.sent-2411-588: The widespread reliance on hyperparameters and constrained optimization techniques introduces significant challenges, particularly in real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-589: While hyperparameters are crucial to the success of many approaches, they must be carefully tuned to avoid overfitting and ensure robustness across varied attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-590: Balancing the bias-variance trade-off in optimization-based approaches is critical for future improvements.
elements.sent-2411-591: Trigger modeling remains a key technique in mitigating backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-592: However, our results suggest that common assumptions about the trigger distribution do not universally hold across all attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-593: This highlights the need for more adaptive approaches that can account for variations in attack types and the properties of input data.
elements.sent-2411-594: A major challenge across all evaluated approaches is the restoration of backdoor-affected classifications, as quantified by RA and RDR.
elements.sent-2411-595: Most approaches, despite reducing attack success rates, fail to fully recover the correct classification of backdoor samples, underscoring the need for future research to focus on improving RDR.
elements.sent-2411-596: In summary, while considerable progress has been made in developing backdoor mitigation strategies, our findings highlight the need for more adaptive, robust, and generalizable solutions.
elements.sent-2411-597: Future research can focus on addressing trade-offs between accuracy, recovery, and computational efficiency, while also exploring new approaches to mitigate backdoor attacks in diverse real-world scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-617: His research interests include trust, mobility, and energy efficiency in networks.

elements.sent-2411-621: Thus, \cite{chai2022awn solve the following minimax optimization problem \begin{equation}\label{awn-min} \begin{split} \min_{\mathbf{m} \in [0, 1]} \biggl\{ \lambda_{1} \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{CE}(\mathcal{D}_{m}, \mathbf{m} \space \odot \theta) +\lambda_{2} \max_{\|\delta\|_{1} \leq \epsilon}\left[ \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{CE}(\widetilde{\mathcal{D}}_{m}, \mathbf{m} \space \odot \theta) \right] + \lambda_{3} \|\mathbf{m} \|_{1} \biggr\}, \end{split}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{1}$, $\lambda_{2}$, and $\lambda_{3}$ are hyperparameters that balance the influence of the three loss terms, and $\|\mathbf{m \|_{1}$ serves an additional regularization term to encourage sparsity.}}
elements.sent-2411-622: Among the conventional fine-tuning approaches (i.e., FST and FT-SAM), only FT-SAM outperforms FT, as indicated by its lower median ASR and RDR, as well as reduced variance, in Fig.~\ref{fig:result-summary .}
elements.sent-2411-623: While FST achieves slightly reduced ASR and ARR relative to FT, underperforms in terms of RDR.
elements.sent-2411-624: Additionally, FST exhibits higher variance in ASR and ARR compared to FT-SAM.
elements.sent-2411-625: NAD demonstrates overall performance comparable to FT but with increased variance in ARR.
elements.sent-2411-626: Compared to FT, i-BAU shows lower performance across all three measures.
elements.sent-2411-627: PBE achieves similar ASR and RDR compared to FT, though it exhibits a higher median ARR and a longer tail.
elements.sent-2411-628: In contrast, SAU demonstrates significant improvement over FT, with a lower median ASR and reduced variance.
elements.sent-2411-629: This is surprising given that BadNets is considered the foundational attack.
